title: Optimistic Online LQR via Intrinsic Rewards
abstract: Optimism in the face of uncertainty is a popular approach to balance exploration and exploitation in reinforcement learning. Here, we consider the online linear quadratic regulator (LQR) problem, i.e., to learn the LQR corresponding to an unknown linear dynamical system by adapting the control policy online based on closed-loop data collected during operation. In this work, we propose Intrinsic Rewards LQR (IR-LQR), an optimistic online LQR algorithm that applies the idea of intrinsic rewards originating from reinforcement learning and the concept of variance regularization to promote uncertainty-driven exploration. IR-LQR retains the structure of a standard LQR synthesis problem by only modifying the cost function, resulting in an intuitively pleasing, simple, computationally cheap, and efficient algorithm. This is in contrast to existing optimistic online LQR formulations that rely on more complicated iterative search algorithms or solve computationally demanding optimization problems. We show that IR-LQR achieves the optimal worst-case regret rate of $\sqrt{T}$, and compare it to various state-of-the-art online LQR algorithms via numerical experiments carried out on an aircraft pitch angle control and an unmanned aerial vehicle example.

sections.heading: IIntroduction
sections.text:
Reinforcement learning (RL) has proven effective in a variety of applications from game playing [ 26 , 27 , 15 ] to robotics [ 24 , 18 ] . Despite its success, it remains challenging to apply reliably in the real world due to sample inefficiency [ 38 , 11 , 16 ] . One of the critical bottlenecks in designing sample efficient RL algorithms is to efficiently navigate the exploration-exploitation tradeoff.

Learning the linear quadratic regulator (LQR) through online interactions with an unknown system has emerged as a standard benchmark to study the efficacy of particular exploration schemes in continuous state and action spaces. Prior investigations have focused on strategies such as optimism in the face of uncertainty, and naive exploration [ 1 , 33 ] . Such approaches either sacrifice the computationally efficiency of LQR synthesis, or explore in an undirected manner. We study the use of uncertainty-based exploration bonuses for which the synthesis problem remains an LQR problem and show that this strategy achieves the optimal T \sqrt{T} regret rate.

Namely, the synthesis problem lying at the heart of the proposed algorithm can be informally written as K t = argmin K J LQR ​ ( K , Θ ^ t , Q ~ t ) \displaystyle K_{t}=\operatorname*{argmin}_{K}J^{\mathrm{LQR}}(K,\hat{\Theta}_{t},\tilde{Q}_{t}) where the informal notation J LQR ​ ( K , Θ ^ t , Q ~ t ) J^{\mathrm{LQR}}(K,\hat{\Theta}_{t},\tilde{Q}_{t}) represents the LQR cost associated with controller K K under the current model estimate Θ ^ t \hat{\Theta}_{t} with a modified weight matrix Q ~ t \tilde{Q}_{t} .

sections.heading: I-BContribution
sections.text:
We present an algorithm for learning the LQR through online interaction. The algorithm uses uncertainty-informed exploration bonuses to modify the control synthesis objective, thereby directing exploration to regions of the state and action space where the dynamics are most uncertain. The exploration bonus is chosen such that the synthesis problem remains an LQR problem, making it computationally efficient. By demonstrating that this synthesis objective is a lower bound on the cost achieved by the optimal controller (and therefore conforms to the OFU principle), we derive a high probability regret bound that scales with the mini-max optimal T \sqrt{T} rate, where T T is the number of interactions with the environment (i.e. time steps). The algorithm is evaluated on an airplane pitch control and an unmanned aerial vehicle example, and compared with alternatives such as naive exploration and other optimistic synthesis procedures. The experiments demonstrate the improved sample efficiency and computational efficiency of the approach.

Outline: The problem we aim to address is detailed in Section II . Section III then presents the proposed method, and we analyze its performance in the form of a high-probability regret bound in Section IV . The results of the numerical experiments are presented in Section V , which is followed by the conclusion in Section VI . The proofs of the theoretical results can be found in the Appendix.

Notation: For vector x x , we denote its Euclidean norm by ‖ x ‖ \|x\| . We use ≻ \succ ( ⪰ \succeq ) to indicate positive (semi)definiteness. For matrix A A , we use ‖ A ‖ 2 \left\|A\right\|_{2} and ‖ A ‖ F \left\|A\right\|_{\mathrm{F}} to denote its spectral and Frobenius norm respectively, and ‖ A ‖ P \left\|A\right\|_{P} represents the weighted Frobenius norm ‖ P 1 / 2 ​ A ‖ F \left\|P^{1/2}A\right\|_{\mathrm{F}} for P ≻ 0 P\succ 0 . Projection onto a convex compact set 𝒮 \mathcal{S} is denoted by Π 𝒮 \Pi_{\mathcal{S}} . Consider the generalized discrete algebraic Riccati equation: P = A ⊤ ​ P ​ A − ( A ⊤ ​ P ​ B + N ) ​ ( B ⊤ ​ P ​ B + R ) − 1 ​ ( B ⊤ ​ P ​ A + N ⊤ ) + Q P=A^{\top}PA-(A^{\top}PB+N)(B^{\top}PB+R)^{-1}(B^{\top}PA+N^{\top})+Q . The unique positive definite solutions of the generalized discrete algebraic Riccati equation and the discrete Lyapunov equation are denoted by DARE ​ ( A , B , Q , R , N ) \textnormal{{DARE}}(A,B,Q,R,N) and DLyap ​ ( A , Q ) \textnormal{{DLyap}}(A,Q) , respectively. The block diagonal matrix with blocks Q Q and R R in its diagonal is denoted by diag ​ ( Q , R ) \mathrm{diag}(Q,R) . We use 𝒪 ~ \tilde{\mathcal{O}} to hide logarithmic factors. For P ⪰ 0 P\succeq 0 with eigendecomposition P = U ​ diag ⁡ ( λ 1 , … , λ n ) ​ U ⊤ P=U\operatorname{diag}(\lambda_{1},\dots,\lambda_{n})U^{\top} and c > 0 c>0 , we define the spectral clipping operator sclip ⁡ ( P , c ) := U ​ diag ⁡ ( min ⁡ { λ 1 , c } , … , min ⁡ { λ n , c } ) ​ U ⊤ . \operatorname{sclip}(P,c):=U\operatorname{diag}\big(\min\{\lambda_{1},c\},\dots,\min\{\lambda_{n},c\}\big)U^{\top}. That is, sclip ⁡ ( P , c ) \operatorname{sclip}(P,c) is obtained by capping the eigenvalues of P P above by c c .

sections.heading: IIProblem Formulation
sections.text:
We consider an unknown linear time invariant dynamical system which evolves according to x t + 1 \displaystyle x_{t+1} = A ⋆ ​ x t + B ⋆ ​ u t + w t , t ≥ 0 \displaystyle=A_{\star}x_{t}+B_{\star}u_{t}+w_{t},t\geq 0 (1) with state x t ∈ ℝ d 𝗑 x_{t}\in\mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathsf{x}}} , input u t ∈ ℝ d 𝗎 u_{t}\in\mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathsf{u}}} , process noise w t ​ ∼ i ​ i ​ d ​ 𝒩 ​ ( 0 , σ 𝗐 2 ​ I ) w_{t}\overset{iid}{\sim}\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_{\mathsf{w}}^{2}I) 1 1 1 To simplify notation we restrict our attention to this simplified setting, but extensions to general Σ 𝗐 ≻ 0 \Sigma_{\mathsf{w}}\succ 0 noise covariances or even sub-Gaussian distributions are straightforward. for σ 𝗐 > 0 \sigma_{\mathsf{w}}>0 , and initial condition x 0 = 0 x_{0}=0 . We assume that we can directly measure the state x t x_{t} and that the pair of unknown matrices ( A ⋆ , B ⋆ ) (A_{\star},B_{\star}) is stabilizable. We will use the shorthand notation Θ ⋆ := [ A ⋆ B ⋆ ] \Theta_{\star}:=\begin{bmatrix}A_{\star}&B_{\star}\end{bmatrix} .

We consider the problem of learning to control ( 1 ) through online interactions. Let the control objective be defined by cost matrices Q ≻ 0 Q\succ 0 and R ≻ 0 R\succ 0 . The learner interacts with the system for T T time steps, and incurs a cumulative cost C T := ∑ t = 0 T − 1 ℓ ​ ( x t , u t ) , ℓ ​ ( x t , u t ) := x t ⊤ ​ Q ​ x t + u t ⊤ ​ R ​ u t . \displaystyle C_{T}:=\sum_{t=0}^{T-1}\ell(x_{t},u_{t}),\quad\ell(x_{t},u_{t}):=x_{t}^{\top}Qx_{t}+u_{t}^{\top}Ru_{t}. The performance of the learner is compared to the cost that could be achieved on average if the agent knew the system in advance. To this end, we define the ergodic LQR cost as J ( K , Θ ) := lim sup T → ∞ 1 T E Θ K [ ∑ t = 0 T − 1 ℓ ( x t , u t ) ] , \displaystyle J(K,\Theta)=\limsup_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{T}\operatorname{\textbf{E}}_{\Theta}^{K}\mathopen{}\left[\sum_{t=0}^{T-1}\ell(x_{t},u_{t})\right]\mathclose{}, (2) where expectation is taken with respect to the process noise, the subscript denotes that the state evolves according to ( 1 ) with Θ = [ A B ] \Theta=\begin{bmatrix}A&B\end{bmatrix} taking the place of Θ ⋆ \Theta_{\star} , and the superscript denotes that the actions are selected according to the controller K K as u t = K ​ x t u_{t}=Kx_{t} . We let K ⋆ = argmin K ∈ ℝ d 𝗎 × d 𝗑 J ​ ( K , Θ ⋆ ) K_{\star}=\operatorname*{argmin}_{K\in\mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathsf{u}}\times d_{\mathsf{x}}}}J(K,\Theta_{\star}) be the optimal controller.

The regret of the learner is defined in the literature [ 1 ] as ℛ T := C T − T ​ J ​ ( K ⋆ , Θ ⋆ ) . \displaystyle\mathcal{R}_{T}:=C_{T}-TJ(K_{\star},\Theta_{\star}). A learner that approaches optimal behavior through the course of its interactions incurs sublinear regret.

To show sublinear regret, we focus our attention on learning optimal behavior starting from a good initial guess. To this end, we introduce an initial set of parameters centered around the nominal estimate Θ ^ 0 := [ A ^ 0 B ^ 0 ] \hat{\Theta}_{0}:=\begin{bmatrix}\hat{A}_{0}&\hat{B}_{0}\end{bmatrix} of radius 1 / λ 1/\lambda : 𝒞 0 := { Θ ∈ ℝ d 𝗑 × ( d 𝗑 + d 𝗎 ) : ∥ Θ − Θ ^ 0 ∥ F ≤ 1 λ } . \displaystyle\mathcal{C}_{0}:=\mathopen{}\left\{\Theta\in\mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathsf{x}}\times(d_{\mathsf{x}}+d_{\mathsf{u}})}:\big\|\Theta-\hat{\Theta}_{0}\big\|_{\mathrm{F}}\leq\frac{1}{\lambda}\right\}\mathclose{}. (3) We assume that this initial set contains the true parameter.

Note that this assumption is not inherently restrictive in the sense that λ \lambda could be arbitrarily small. However, algorithmic certificates will require a large enough λ \lambda . Without prior knowledge of this set, the learner could use warm-up schemes that achieve a coarse model of the system and an initial stabilizing controller, as in [ 12 ] .

Consider a more general version of ( 2 ), where the positive definite cost to be minimized also contains cross terms: ℓ ​ ( x , u ) = x ⊤ ​ Q ​ x + u ⊤ ​ R ​ u + 2 ​ x ⊤ ​ N ​ u \ell(x,u)=x^{\top}Qx+u^{\top}Ru+2x^{\top}Nu . Then, for a known system, the minimizer is the LQR: K ​ ( A , B , Q , R , N ) \displaystyle K(A,B,Q,R,N) := − ( B ⊤ ​ P ​ B + R ) − 1 ​ ( B ⊤ ​ P ​ A + N ) \displaystyle:=-(B^{\top}PB+R)^{-1}(B^{\top}PA+N) P \displaystyle P = DARE ​ ( A , B , Q , R , N ) . \displaystyle=\textnormal{{DARE}}(A,B,Q,R,N). (4) Our method will rely on the solution to such generalized LQR problems. The closed-form expression for the LQR controller via the Riccati equation makes the synthesis problem for a known dynamical system computationally efficient, and scalable to high-dimensional systems. In the sequel, we show that the learner may achieve sublinear regret using the principle of optimism in the face of uncertainty by solving LQR synthesis problems with a modified cost.

sections.heading: IIIProposed Method
sections.text: We consider a learner that interacts with the system under a static state-feedback controller and periodically updates the controller gain using data collected from past interactions. To update the gain, the learner first estimates the unknown system parameters and then solves a control synthesis problem with a subtracted exploration bonus.

sections.heading: III-AParameter Estimation
sections.text:
To estimate the parameters of the system at an arbitrary time t t , the learner uses the data 𝒟 t = { ( x k , u k , x k + 1 ) } k = 0 t − 1 \mathcal{D}_{t}=\mathopen{}\left\{(x_{k},u_{k},x_{k+1})\right\}\mathclose{}_{k=0}^{t-1} to fit an estimate for Θ ⋆ \Theta_{\star} using the projected regularized least squares estimator Θ ^ t = Π 𝒞 0 ​ [ ( ∑ k = 1 t x k ​ [ x k − 1 u k − 1 ] ⊤ + λ ​ Θ ^ 0 ) ​ V t − 1 ] , \displaystyle\hat{\Theta}_{t}=\Pi_{\mathcal{C}_{0}}\left[\left(\sum_{k=1}^{t}x_{k}\begin{bmatrix}x_{k-1}\\
u_{k-1}\end{bmatrix}^{\top}+\lambda\hat{\Theta}_{0}\right)V^{-1}_{t}\right], (5) where V t V_{t} denotes the empirical covariance matrix V t = λ ​ I + ∑ k = 0 t − 1 [ x k u k ] ​ [ x k u k ] ⊤ , \displaystyle V_{t}=\lambda I+\sum_{k=0}^{t-1}\begin{bmatrix}x_{k}\\
u_{k}\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}x_{k}\\
u_{k}\end{bmatrix}^{\top}, (6) and the regularizer λ \lambda is the same variable that appears in the definition of 𝒞 0 \mathcal{C}_{0} in ( 3 ). This estimator is commonly used in online LQR (cf. [ 1 , 9 , 3 ] ) as it achieves the best fit to the data 𝒟 t \mathcal{D}_{t} in a (regularized) least-squares sense.
The regularization term is equivalent to placing an independent Gaussian prior on the entries of Θ \Theta centered at Θ ^ 0 \hat{\Theta}_{0} , with variance σ 𝗐 2 / λ \sigma_{\mathsf{w}}^{2}/\lambda for each entry.
Assumption II.1 is taken into account by projecting the estimate onto 𝒞 0 \mathcal{C}_{0} .

The gap between this estimate and the ground truth is known to be bounded in terms of the empirical covariance matrix as follows.

sections.heading: III-BController Synthesis
sections.text:
The learner uses the estimate Θ ^ t \hat{\Theta}_{t} to synthesize a controller to apply in subsequent time steps. It is well known that if the learner synthesizes and deploys a certainty equivalent policy greedily as u t = K ​ ( A ^ t , B ^ t , Q , R , 0 ) ​ x t u_{t}=K(\hat{A}_{t},\hat{B}_{t},Q,R,0)x_{t} , it can get stuck in local minima in which the estimates of A ^ t \hat{A}_{t} and B ^ t \hat{B}_{t} do not improve [ 33 ] . The learner then fails to achieve sublinear regret. This motivates approaches such as dithering that inject random noise at the actuation channel, as analyzed in [ 33 ] in the online LQR setting; and more broadly it touches upon the concept of persistency of excitation in adaptive control [ 28 ] . Such exploration is undirected and continuously excites parameter directions also where the optimal controller already provides exploration. Optimistic approaches combat this inefficiency by using the uncertainty in the parameter estimates informed by Lemma III.1 to guide exploration more naturally. Unfortunately, they are typically computationally challenging. We therefore propose to use the estimate Θ ^ t \hat{\Theta}_{t} to synthesize an LQR controller with an exploration bonus.

To this end, we define a modified control objective J ~ \displaystyle\tilde{J} ( K , Θ , 𝒱 ) := \displaystyle(K,\Theta,\mathcal{V}):= (10) lim sup T → ∞ 1 T E Θ K [ ∑ t = 0 T − 1 [ x t u t ] ⊤ ( [ Q R ] − 𝒱 ) [ x t u t ] ] , \displaystyle\limsup_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{T}\operatorname{\textbf{E}}_{\Theta}^{K}\mathopen{}\left[\sum_{t=0}^{T-1}\begin{bmatrix}x_{t}\\
u_{t}\end{bmatrix}^{\top}\left(\begin{bmatrix}Q&\\
&R\end{bmatrix}-\mathcal{V}\right)\begin{bmatrix}x_{t}\\
u_{t}\end{bmatrix}\right]\mathclose{}, which can be understood as a generalized LQR problem where the cost has been modified by the matrix 𝒱 \mathcal{V} . We will refer to the matrix 𝒱 \mathcal{V} as the exploration bonus. In particular, after a sufficient amount of data has been collected by some time step t t , we synthesize the controller K t = argmin K J ~ ​ ( K , Θ ^ t , 𝒱 t ) , \displaystyle K_{t}=\operatorname*{argmin}_{K}\tilde{J}(K,\hat{\Theta}_{t},\mathcal{V}_{t}), (11) where 𝒱 t := sclip ⁡ ( g t ​ V t − 1 , c ​ ( λ ) ) . \displaystyle\mathcal{V}_{t}:=\operatorname{sclip}\left(g_{t}V_{t}^{-1},c(\lambda)\right). (12)

Here, g t > 0 g_{t}>0 is an appropriately chosen weight, and c ​ ( λ ) > 0 c(\lambda)>0 depends on problem-specific constants and λ \lambda . Intuitively, this construction encourages exploration in directions promoted by g t ​ V t − 1 g_{t}V_{t}^{-1} , that is, directions of high empirical variance, while ensuring that the cost is positive definite by using the spectral clipping operator sclip \operatorname{sclip} .
Note that this corresponds to minimizing an LQR cost function that is modified such that the state and input penalties are reduced along directions of the state and input space where past data provides less information (i.e., where the uncertainty encoded by V t − 1 V_{t}^{-1} is large).
In other words, this cost modification promotes exploration, commonly referred to as an intrinsic reward in reinforcement learning [ 6 , 37 ] . The synthesis problem ( 11 ) is efficiently solvable by writing it as in ( 4 ).

A further insight is that the modified cost ( 10 ) with 𝒱 t \mathcal{V}_{t} follows the principle of optimism in the face of uncertainty (OFU) [ 1 ] , and thus we will refer to this cost as the optimistic cost, and the corresponding optimization problem ( 11 ) as the optimistic synthesis problem. One of our main technical contributions is to show that the optimistic cost is a lower bound on the ergodic LQR cost ( 2 ) achieved by the optimal controller. This result relies on the following two lemmas. First, we state that a good enough initial estimate (i.e. small enough 𝒞 0 \mathcal{C}_{0} ) results in a set of controllers 𝒦 \mathcal{K} such that every element of 𝒦 \mathcal{K} stabilizes every element of 𝒞 0 \mathcal{C}_{0} .

Next, we show that the optimistic synthesis problem ( 11 ) results in controllers that lie in the set 𝒦 \mathcal{K} .

Finally, we can state the aforementioned lower bound result.

The proofs of Lemmas III.2 - III.3 and Theorem III.1 can be found in Appendix VI-A , VI-B , and VI-C .

sections.heading: III-CAlgorithm and Discussion
sections.text:
Building on the observation that this synthesis procedure is optimistic, we draw inspiration from existing methods following the OFU principle [ 1 , 9 , 3 ] to devise an algorithm with sublinear regret guarantees. The proposed algorithm (IR-LQR) is presented in Algorithm 1 .

Algorithm 1 is non-episodic in the sense that it is applied to a single continuous trajectory of the system without requiring restarts. However, as is customary in online LQR [ 1 , 9 , 3 , 33 ] , it operates in epochs , during which the control policy is kept fixed. After observing the current state x t x_{t} and updating the empirical covariance matrix V t V_{t} , IR-LQR checks whether sufficient new information has been collected since the start of the current epoch (denoted by time index τ \tau ). In particular, the criterion is that the information gain [ 32 ] difference I ​ ( Θ ; 𝒟 t ) − I ​ ( Θ ; 𝒟 τ ) I(\Theta;\mathcal{D}_{t})-I(\Theta;\mathcal{D}_{\tau}) exceeds a constant threshold of 1 / 2 1/2 bits, which can be equivalently expressed as det ⁡ ( V t ) > 2 ​ det ⁡ ( V τ ) \operatorname{\mathrm{det}}(V_{t})>2\operatorname{\mathrm{det}}(V_{\tau}) in our setting. When this criterion is met and the epoch length exceeds a lower bound τ ¯ ​ ( m , t ) \underline{\tau}(m,t) , a new controller is synthesized according to ( 11 ), and a new epoch begins. The minimum epoch length is set long enough to avoid switching too frequently, and it is defined in ( 29 ) in the Appendix. Such a combination of an information gain criterion and a minimum epoch length for online LQR has also been used in [ 22 ] .

Note that IR-LQR solves optimization problem ( 11 ) to synthesize the controller, which reduces to a generalized LQR problem ( 4 ) provided that diag ​ ( Q , R ) − 𝒱 t ≻ 0 \mathrm{diag}(Q,R)-\mathcal{V}_{t}\succ 0 . In this case, the solution can be computed very efficiently by solving a generalized Riccati equation. Since c ​ ( λ ) = 𝒪 ​ ( 1 / λ ) c(\lambda)=\mathcal{O}(1/\lambda) , there exists a large enough λ \lambda such that diag ​ ( Q , R ) − 𝒱 t ⪰ diag ​ ( Q , R ) − c ​ ( λ ) ​ I ≻ 0 \mathrm{diag}(Q,R)-\mathcal{V}_{t}\succeq\mathrm{diag}(Q,R)-c(\lambda)I\succ 0 , fulfilling the positive definite cost requirement. In other words, IR-LQR is a conceptually simple and computationally cheap algorithm that is easy to implement. This is in contrast to other optimistic online LQR methods, which require solving non-convex or semidefinite optimization problems in the case of [ 1 ] and [ 9 ] , respectively, or complicated search algorithms [ 3 ] .

sections.heading: IVRegret Analysis
sections.text:
We now proceed by stating one of the main results of the paper, which is a high probability bound on the regret of the proposed IR-LQR algorithm (Algorithm 1 ).

Prior work has shown that ℛ T = 𝒪 ~ ​ ( T ) \mathcal{R}_{T}=\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{T}) is the best attainable rate in general for the online LQR problem [ 33 , 43 ] . This lower bound has been achieved up to problem constants and polylogarithmic terms by a variety of existing methods including other optimism-based approaches [ 1 , 9 , 3 ] , as well as methods following certainty equivalent design combined with naive, undirected exploration [ 25 , 33 ] .

However, to the authors’ best knowledge, this is the first work that shows that ℛ T = 𝒪 ~ ​ ( T ) \mathcal{R}_{T}=\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{T}) can be achieved by using an exploration bonus which maintains the structure of the LQR problem. Most closely related is the method proposed in [ 9 ] that modifies the SDP formulation of the LQR, leading to a computationally harder synthesis problem, and more complicated analysis. Beyond the LQR problem, the regret of using similar intrinsic rewards for model-based RL has been shown to scale with T \sqrt{T} for nonlinear dynamics represented by Gaussian processes [ 37 ] ; however, these results rely on a synthesis oracle. We show that in the linear setting, a modification of this results in synthesis problems that are generalized LQR problems. A further advantage of IR-LQR compared to other optimistic online LQR methods is that the required accuracy of the initial estimate Θ ^ 0 \hat{\Theta}_{0} is independent of T T , whereas the initial estimate for [ 3 ] and [ 9 ] needs to be accurate within 1 / λ = 𝒪 ​ ( 1 / log ⁡ T ) 1/\lambda=\mathcal{O}(1/\log T) and 1 / λ = 𝒪 ​ ( 1 / T ) 1/\lambda=\mathcal{O}(1/\sqrt{T}) , respectively. A summary of this discussion can be found in Table I .

sections.heading: VNumerical Experiments
sections.text:
In this section, we compare IR-LQR with state-of-the-art online LQR algorithms on an aircraft pitch angle control example 4 4 4 The example was taken from “Control Tutorials for Matlab and Simulink”, University of Michigan https://ctms.engin.umich.edu/CTMS/?example=AircraftPitch&section=SystemModeling , and a UAV control problem taken from [ 22 ] .
The code to reproduce the results can be found online, along with several additional benchmark problems. 5 5 5 https://github.com/lenarttreven/lqr_research The experiments were carried out using Python on a machine equipped with an Apple M1 processor and 32 GB of RAM, The considered methods are: (1) Intrinsic Reward LQR (IR-LQR, Algorithm 1 ), (2) Certainty Equivalence Control with Persistent Excitation (CEC+PE [ 34 ] ), (3) Optimistic Semidefinite Programming for LQ Control (OSLO [ 9 ] ), (4) Optimistic LQR with Lagrangian Relaxation (LagLQ [ 3 ] ), and (5) Thompson Sampling LQR (TS [ 2 ] ).

We carried out hyperparameter tuning for all of the algorithms. We sampled 40 random noise sequence realizations and ran the experiments with horizon T = 200 T=200 . The results are summarized in Figure 1 .
The plots on the left show the cumulative regret suffered by the methods. Every method achieved a sublinear regret rate as predicted by the theory, and their performances are comparable, although IR-LQR achieves the lowest median regret among them in both cases.

We also compared the algorithms in terms of computational load. As expected, the methods that rely on solving more complicated synthesis problems (LagLQ, OSLO) are computationally significantly more expensive compared to those that rely on LQR synthesis problems (IR-LQR, TS, CEC+PE): while the number of controller updates are almost identical across them, the time it takes to carry out a single controller update is an order of magnitude slower for the former class of methods compared to the latter.

sections.heading: VIConclusion
sections.text: We proposed IR-LQR, an algorithm based on the optimism in the face of uncertainty principle to solve the online LQR problem. Unlike existing optimism-based methods, our method is able to retain the structure of the original LQR synthesis problem, resulting in an intuitively pleasing, conceptually simple, computationally cheap, and efficient algorithm. We achieved this by leveraging the idea of intrinsic rewards from the field of reinforcement learning. Additionally, it does not require persistently exciting noise injection to learn the optimal controller. We proved that IR-LQR matches the optimal worst-case regret rate of T \sqrt{T} , validated the theoretical findings via numerical experiments, and compared IR-LQR to several state-of-the-art online LQR algorithms.

sections.heading: Appendix
sections.text:
For the proofs, we introduce the following notation: Σ ~ Θ K := [ I K ] ​ Σ Θ K ​ [ I K ] ⊤ , Σ Θ K := DLyap ​ ( Θ ​ [ I K ] , σ 𝗐 2 ​ I ) . \displaystyle\tilde{\Sigma}^{K}_{\Theta}:=\begin{bmatrix}I\\
K\end{bmatrix}\Sigma^{K}_{\Theta}\begin{bmatrix}I\\
K\end{bmatrix}^{\top},\ \Sigma^{K}_{\Theta}:=\textnormal{{DLyap}}(\Theta\begin{bmatrix}I\\
K\end{bmatrix},\sigma_{\mathsf{w}}^{2}I). Additionally, we remark that the LQR costs can be equivalently written as J ​ ( K , Θ ) \displaystyle J(K,\Theta) = tr ⁡ ( diag ​ ( Q , R ) ​ Σ ~ Θ K ) \displaystyle=\operatorname{\mathrm{tr}}(\mathrm{diag}(Q,R)\tilde{\Sigma}^{K}_{\Theta}) = tr ⁡ ( ( Q + K ⊤ ​ R ​ K ) ​ Σ Θ K ) , \displaystyle=\operatorname{\mathrm{tr}}((Q+K^{\top}RK)\Sigma^{K}_{\Theta}), J ~ ​ ( K , Θ , 𝒱 ) \displaystyle\tilde{J}(K,\Theta,\mathcal{V}) = tr ⁡ ( ( diag ​ ( Q , R ) − 𝒱 ) ​ Σ ~ Θ K ) . \displaystyle=\operatorname{\mathrm{tr}}((\mathrm{diag}(Q,R)-\mathcal{V})\tilde{\Sigma}^{K}_{\Theta}).

sections.heading: VI-AProof of LemmaIII.2
sections.text: Since A ^ 0 + B ^ 0 ​ K 0 \hat{A}_{0}+\hat{B}_{0}K_{0} is Schur stable and the set of Schur stable matrices is open, there exist ϵ > 0 \epsilon>0 and ρ ¯ ∈ ( 0 , 1 ) \bar{\rho}\in(0,1) such that ‖ M − ( A ^ 0 + B ^ 0 ​ K 0 ) ‖ 2 ≤ ϵ \|M-(\hat{A}_{0}+\hat{B}_{0}K_{0})\|_{2}\leq\epsilon implies ρ ​ ( M ) ≤ ρ ¯ \rho(M)\leq\bar{\rho} . For any Θ = [ A ​ B ] ∈ 𝒞 0 \Theta=[A\ B]\in\mathcal{C}_{0} and K ∈ 𝒦 K\in\mathcal{K} , ‖ A + B ​ K − ( A ^ 0 + B ^ 0 ​ K 0 ) ‖ 2 \displaystyle\big\|A+BK-(\hat{A}_{0}+\hat{B}_{0}K_{0})\big\|_{2} ≤ ‖ A − A ^ 0 ‖ 2 + ‖ ( B − B ^ 0 ) ​ K ‖ 2 + ‖ B ^ 0 ​ ( K − K 0 ) ‖ 2 \displaystyle\leq\big\|A-\hat{A}_{0}\big\|_{2}+\big\|(B-\hat{B}_{0})K\big\|_{2}+\big\|\hat{B}_{0}(K-K_{0})\big\|_{2} and therefore ‖ A + B ​ K − ( A ^ 0 + B ^ 0 ​ K 0 ) ‖ 2 ≤ 1 + ‖ K 0 ‖ F + r K λ + ‖ B ^ 0 ‖ 2 ​ r K . \displaystyle\big\|A+BK-(\hat{A}_{0}+\hat{B}_{0}K_{0})\big\|_{2}\leq\frac{1+\left\|K_{0}\right\|_{\mathrm{F}}+r_{K}}{\lambda}+\big\|\hat{B}_{0}\big\|_{2}r_{K}. Choose r K r_{K} and then λ ⋆ \lambda_{\star} so that the right-hand side is at most ϵ \epsilon for all λ ≥ λ ⋆ \lambda\geq\lambda_{\star} . Hence every matrix in 𝒮 := { A + B K : Θ ∈ 𝒞 0 , K ∈ 𝒦 } \displaystyle\mathcal{S}:=\mathopen{}\left\{A+BK:\Theta\in\mathcal{C}_{0},\ K\in\mathcal{K}\right\}\mathclose{} is Schur stable. Since 𝒮 \mathcal{S} is compact, by continuity of the discrete Lyapunov equation there exist P ≻ 0 P\succ 0 and η > 0 \eta>0 such that M ⊤ ​ P ​ M ⪯ ( 1 − η ) ​ P , ∀ M ∈ 𝒮 . \displaystyle M^{\top}PM\preceq(1-\eta)P,\qquad\forall M\in\mathcal{S}. Consequently, ‖ M k ‖ 2 ≤ κ ​ ( P ) ​ ( 1 − η ) k / 2 , ∀ M ∈ 𝒮 , ∀ k ≥ 0 . \displaystyle\left\|M^{k}\right\|_{2}\leq\sqrt{\kappa(P)}(1-\eta)^{k/2},\qquad\forall M\in\mathcal{S},\ \forall k\geq 0. The claim follows with C = d 𝗑 ​ κ ​ ( P ) C=\sqrt{d_{\mathsf{x}}\kappa(P)} and ρ = 1 − η \rho=\sqrt{1-\eta} . ■ \blacksquare

sections.heading: VI-BProof of LemmaIII.3
sections.text: Since Θ ^ τ m ∈ 𝒞 0 \hat{\Theta}_{\tau_{m}}\in\mathcal{C}_{0} , we have ‖ Θ ^ τ m − Θ ^ 0 ‖ F ≤ 1 / λ . \displaystyle\big\|\hat{\Theta}_{\tau_{m}}-\hat{\Theta}_{0}\big\|_{\mathrm{F}}\leq 1/\lambda. Moreover, by construction of the clipped exploration bonus, ‖ 𝒱 τ m ‖ 2 ≤ c ​ ( λ ) . \displaystyle\left\|\mathcal{V}_{\tau_{m}}\right\|_{2}\leq c(\lambda). Because c ​ ( λ ) = 𝒪 ​ ( 1 / λ ) c(\lambda)=\mathcal{O}(1/\lambda) and the stabilizing generalized LQR controller depends continuously on the problem data in a neighborhood of ( A ^ 0 , B ^ 0 , Q , R , 0 ) (\hat{A}_{0},\hat{B}_{0},Q,R,0) , there exist constants L Θ , L V L_{\Theta},L_{V} such that ‖ K τ m − K 0 ‖ F \displaystyle\left\|K_{\tau_{m}}-K_{0}\right\|_{\mathrm{F}} ≤ L Θ ​ ‖ Θ ^ τ m − Θ ^ 0 ‖ F + L V ​ ‖ 𝒱 τ m ‖ 2 \displaystyle\leq L_{\Theta}\big\|\hat{\Theta}_{\tau_{m}}-\hat{\Theta}_{0}\big\|_{\mathrm{F}}+L_{V}\left\|\mathcal{V}_{\tau_{m}}\right\|_{2} ≤ L Θ / λ + L V ​ c ​ ( λ ) , \displaystyle\leq L_{\Theta}/\lambda+L_{V}c(\lambda), see also [ 5 , Lemma 3] . Choosing λ \lambda sufficiently large yields ‖ K τ m − K 0 ‖ F ≤ r K \left\|K_{\tau_{m}}-K_{0}\right\|_{\mathrm{F}}\leq r_{K} , hence K τ m ∈ 𝒦 K_{\tau_{m}}\in\mathcal{K} . ■ \blacksquare

sections.heading: VI-CProof of TheoremIII.1and further auxiliary results
sections.text:
The proof relies on the following result on the perturbation of Lyapunov equations:

Lemma VI.1 , enables us to bound the difference in the cost of a given controller on the true system and the estimated system by the exploration bonus, which is formally stated in the following lemma:

Note that this result immediately implies Theorem III.1 , i.e., that the optimistic cost is a lower bound on the original cost.

Before proceeding with the proof of Theorem IV.1 , we introduce the notation τ m \tau_{m} to denote the time step of the start of epoch m ∈ { 0 , … , M − 1 } m\in\{0,\dots,M-1\} with τ 0 = 0 \tau_{0}=0 , where the word ‘epoch’ refers to the set of time indices for which the controller K t K_{t} has been kept fixed because the inequality in line 5 of Algorithm 1 has not been satisfied. For simplicity, we assume that τ M = T \tau_{M}=T , i.e., the experiment is run until the end of epoch M M , but the arguments presented here can also be generalized to arbitrary T T . We also define the following quantities:

w ¯ t := max 0 ≤ k ≤ t ⁡ ‖ w k ‖ , x ¯ t := max 0 ≤ k ≤ t ⁡ ‖ x k ‖ , Σ ¯ := max K ∈ 𝒦 ⁡ ‖ Σ Θ ⋆ K ‖ 2 , \displaystyle\bar{w}_{t}:=\max_{0\leq k\leq t}\left\|w_{k}\right\|,\,\bar{x}_{t}:=\max_{0\leq k\leq t}\left\|x_{k}\right\|,\,\bar{\Sigma}:=\max_{K\in\mathcal{K}}\left\|\Sigma^{K}_{\Theta_{\star}}\right\|_{2}, P ¯ := max K ∈ 𝒦 ⁡ ‖ DLyap ​ ( ( A ⋆ + B ⋆ ​ K ) ⊤ , Q + K ⊤ ​ R ​ K ) ‖ 2 . \displaystyle\bar{P}:=\max_{K\in\mathcal{K}}\left\|\textnormal{{DLyap}}((A_{\star}+B_{\star}K)^{\top},Q+K^{\top}RK)\right\|_{2}. (22)

Consider the success event ℰ := ℰ ID ∩ ℰ noise ∩ ⋂ m = 0 M − 1 ℰ m conc , \displaystyle\mathcal{E}:=\mathcal{E}^{\mathrm{ID}}\cap\mathcal{E}^{\mathrm{noise}}\cap\bigcap_{m=0}^{M-1}\mathcal{E}_{m}^{\mathrm{conc}}, where ℰ ID \displaystyle\mathcal{E}^{\mathrm{ID}} := { ∥ Θ ^ t − Θ ⋆ ∥ V t ≤ β t ( δ ID ) , ∀ t ≥ 0 } , \displaystyle:=\mathopen{}\left\{\big\|\hat{\Theta}_{t}-\Theta_{\star}\big\|_{V_{t}}\leq\beta_{t}(\delta^{\mathrm{ID}}),\forall t\geq 0\right\}\mathclose{}, ℰ noise \displaystyle\mathcal{E}^{\mathrm{noise}} := { w ¯ t ≤ 4 σ 𝗐 d 𝗑 ​ log ⁡ ( π 2 ​ t 3 / ( 6 ​ δ noise ) ) , ∀ t ≥ 0 } , \displaystyle:=\mathopen{}\left\{\bar{w}_{t}\leq 4\sigma_{\mathsf{w}}\sqrt{d_{\mathsf{x}}\log(\pi^{2}t^{3}/(6\delta^{\mathrm{noise}}))},\,\forall t\geq 0\right\}\mathclose{}, and ℰ m conc := { ∥ ( τ m + 1 − τ m ) \displaystyle\mathcal{E}_{m}^{\mathrm{conc}}:=\bigg\{\big\|(\tau_{m+1}-\tau_{m}) Σ Θ ⋆ K τ m − ∑ t = τ m τ m + 1 − 1 x t ​ x t ⊤ ∥ 2 \displaystyle\Sigma^{K_{\tau_{m}}}_{\Theta_{\star}}-\sum_{t=\tau_{m}}^{\tau_{m+1}-1}x_{t}x_{t}^{\top}\big\|_{2} ≤ α ( δ m , τ m + 1 ) τ m + 1 − τ m } , \displaystyle\leq\alpha(\delta_{m},\tau_{m+1})\sqrt{\tau_{m+1}-\tau_{m}}\bigg\}, where α ​ ( δ , t ) \displaystyle\alpha(\delta,t) := C 2 ​ ( x ¯ t 2 + Σ ¯ 1 − ρ 2 + 2 ​ w ¯ t ​ x ¯ t ( 1 − ρ ) 2 ) \displaystyle=C^{2}\left(\frac{\bar{x}_{t}^{2}+\bar{\Sigma}}{1-\rho^{2}}+\frac{2\bar{w}_{t}\bar{x}_{t}}{(1-\rho)^{2}}\right) (23) + ( α ~ ​ ( δ ) 2 + α ~ ​ ( δ ) ) ​ Σ ¯ , \displaystyle\quad+(\tilde{\alpha}(\delta)^{2}+\tilde{\alpha}(\delta))\bar{\Sigma}, α ~ ​ ( δ ) \displaystyle\tilde{\alpha}(\delta) := C 1 − ρ ​ ( 576 ​ d 𝗑 ​ log ⁡ ( 18 / δ ) ) . \displaystyle=\frac{C}{1-\rho}\left(\sqrt{576d_{\mathsf{x}}\log(18/\delta)}\right).

Armed with this result, we can state that the success event ℰ \mathcal{E} occurs with high probability.

sections.heading: VI-DProof of TheoremIV.1
sections.text:
Lemmas VI.5 and VI.6 will be useful to complete the proof of Theorem IV.1 :

Using the above bounds on the state, we can define a bound on α \alpha from ( 23 ) that does not depend on the realization of the noise or the sequence of controllers that is played: α ¯ ​ ( m , t ) := \displaystyle\bar{\alpha}(m,t):= 3 C 2 ( ( 160 ​ P ¯ 2 ​ ( ‖ B ⋆ ‖ 2 2 + 1 ) ​ σ 𝗐 ​ d 𝗑 ​ log ⁡ ( π 2 ​ t 3 / ( 6 ​ δ ) ) ) 2 + Σ ¯ ( 1 − ρ ) 2 \displaystyle 3C^{2}\bigg(\frac{(160\bar{P}^{2}(\left\|B_{\star}\right\|_{2}^{2}+1)\sigma_{\mathsf{w}}\sqrt{d_{\mathsf{x}}\log(\pi^{2}t^{3}/(6\delta))})^{2}+\bar{\Sigma}}{(1-\rho)^{2}} + ( α ~ ( 2 δ / ( π 2 m 2 ) ) 2 + α ~ ( 2 δ / ( π 2 m 2 ) ) Σ ¯ , \displaystyle\quad+(\tilde{\alpha}(2\delta/(\pi^{2}m^{2}))^{2}+\tilde{\alpha}(2\delta/(\pi^{2}m^{2}))\bar{\Sigma}, (28) which can be used to compute the epoch lower bound: τ ¯ ​ ( m , t ) := max ⁡ { log ( 1 − 1 / ( 2 ​ P ¯ ) ) ⁡ ( 1 8 ​ P ¯ ) + 1 , 4 ​ α ¯ ​ ( m , t ) 2 σ 𝗐 4 } . \displaystyle\underline{\tau}(m,t)\!:=\!\max\bigg\{\log_{(1-1/(2\bar{P}))}\left(\frac{1}{8\bar{P}}\right)\!+\!1,\frac{4\bar{\alpha}(m,t)^{2}}{\sigma_{\mathsf{w}}^{4}}\bigg\}. (29)

An immediate consequence of Lemma VI.5 is the following result.

The last part of the proof of Theorem IV.1 is to show that as long as the success event ℰ \mathcal{E} holds, the regret is of order 𝒪 ~ ​ ( T ) \tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{T}) . Defining Δ m = ∑ t = τ m τ m + 1 − 1 ℓ ​ ( x t , u t ) − ( τ m + 1 − τ m ) ​ J ​ ( K τ m , Θ ⋆ ) , \displaystyle\Delta_{m}=\sum_{t=\tau_{m}}^{\tau_{m+1}-1}\ell(x_{t},u_{t})-(\tau_{m+1}-\tau_{m})J(K_{\tau_{m}},\Theta_{\star}), allows us to rewrite the cumulative regret as ℛ T \displaystyle\mathcal{R}_{T} = ∑ m = 0 M − 1 ( ∑ t = τ m τ m + 1 − 1 ℓ ​ ( x t , u t ) − ( τ m + 1 − τ m ) ​ J ​ ( K ⋆ , Θ ⋆ ) ) \displaystyle=\sum_{m=0}^{M-1}\left(\sum_{t=\tau_{m}}^{\tau_{m+1}-1}\ell(x_{t},u_{t})-(\tau_{m+1}-\tau_{m})J(K_{\star},\Theta_{\star})\right) = ∑ m = 0 M − 1 ( Δ i + ( τ m + 1 − τ i ) ​ ( J ​ ( K τ m , Θ ⋆ ) − J ​ ( K ⋆ , Θ ⋆ ) ) ) , \displaystyle=\sum_{m=0}^{M-1}\left(\Delta_{i}+(\tau_{m+1}-\tau_{i})(J(K_{\tau_{m}},\Theta_{\star})-J(K_{\star},\Theta_{\star}))\right), where J ( K τ m \displaystyle J(K_{\tau_{m}} , Θ ⋆ ) − J ( K ⋆ , Θ ⋆ ) ≤ ( ∗ ) J ( K τ m , Θ ⋆ ) − J ~ ( K ⋆ , Θ ^ τ m , 𝒱 τ m ) \displaystyle,\Theta_{\star})-J(K_{\star},\Theta_{\star})\stackrel{{\scriptstyle(*)}}{{\leq}}J(K_{\tau_{m}},\Theta_{\star})-\tilde{J}(K_{\star},\hat{\Theta}_{\tau_{m}},\mathcal{V}_{\tau_{m}}) ≤ J ​ ( K τ m , Θ ⋆ ) − J ~ ​ ( K τ m , Θ ^ τ m , 𝒱 τ m ) \displaystyle\leq J(K_{\tau_{m}},\Theta_{\star})-\tilde{J}(K_{\tau_{m}},\hat{\Theta}_{\tau_{m}},\mathcal{V}_{\tau_{m}}) ≤ J ​ ( K τ m , Θ ⋆ ) − J ​ ( K τ m , Θ ^ τ m ) + tr ⁡ ( 𝒱 τ m ​ Σ ~ Θ ^ τ m K τ m ) , \displaystyle\leq J(K_{\tau_{m}},\Theta_{\star})-J(K_{\tau_{m}},\hat{\Theta}_{\tau_{m}})+\operatorname{\mathrm{tr}}(\mathcal{V}_{\tau_{m}}\tilde{\Sigma}^{K_{\tau_{m}}}_{\hat{\Theta}_{\tau_{m}}}), where ( ∗ ) (*) is due to Theorem III.1 . Therefore, the regret can be decomposed as ℛ T ≤ ℛ T ( 1 ) + ℛ T ( 2 ) + ℛ T ( 3 ) , \displaystyle\mathcal{R}_{T}\leq\mathcal{R}_{T}^{(1)}+\mathcal{R}_{T}^{(2)}+\mathcal{R}_{T}^{(3)}, with ℛ T ( 1 ) \displaystyle\mathcal{R}^{(1)}_{T} = ∑ i = 0 M − 1 Δ m , \displaystyle=\sum_{i=0}^{M-1}\Delta_{m}, ℛ T ( 2 ) \displaystyle\mathcal{R}^{(2)}_{T} = ∑ i = 0 M − 1 ( τ m + 1 − τ m ) ​ ( J ​ ( K τ m , Θ ⋆ ) − J ​ ( K τ m , Θ ^ τ m ) ) , \displaystyle=\sum_{i=0}^{M-1}(\tau_{m+1}-\tau_{m})(J(K_{\tau_{m}},\Theta_{\star})-J(K_{\tau_{m}},\hat{\Theta}_{\tau_{m}})), ℛ T ( 3 ) \displaystyle\mathcal{R}^{(3)}_{T} = ∑ i = 0 M − 1 ( τ m + 1 − τ m ) ​ tr ⁡ ( 𝒱 τ m ​ Σ ~ Θ ^ τ m K τ m ) . \displaystyle=\sum_{i=0}^{M-1}(\tau_{m+1}-\tau_{m})\operatorname{\mathrm{tr}}(\mathcal{V}_{\tau_{m}}\tilde{\Sigma}^{K_{\tau_{m}}}_{\hat{\Theta}_{\tau_{m}}}).

In the following, we bound the three regret components. 1. Bounding ℛ T ( 1 ) \mathcal{R}^{(1)}_{T} . Δ m = − ( τ m + 1 − τ m ) ​ J ​ ( K τ m , Θ ⋆ ) + ∑ t = τ m τ m + 1 − 1 ℓ ​ ( x t , u t ) \displaystyle\Delta_{m}=-(\tau_{m+1}-\tau_{m})J(K_{\tau_{m}},\Theta_{\star})+\sum_{t=\tau_{m}}^{\tau_{m+1}-1}\ell(x_{t},u_{t}) = − ( τ m + 1 − τ m ) ​ J ​ ( K τ m , Θ ⋆ ) \displaystyle=-(\tau_{m+1}-\tau_{m})J(K_{\tau_{m}},\Theta_{\star}) + ∑ t = τ m τ m + 1 − 1 tr ⁡ ( [ Q R ] ​ [ I K τ m ] ​ x t ​ x t ⊤ ​ [ I K τ m ] ⊤ ) \displaystyle+\sum_{t=\tau_{m}}^{\tau_{m+1}-1}\operatorname{\mathrm{tr}}\left(\begin{bmatrix}Q&\\
&R\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}I\\
K_{\tau_{m}}\end{bmatrix}x_{t}x_{t}^{\top}\begin{bmatrix}I\\
K_{\tau_{m}}\end{bmatrix}^{\top}\right) = − ( τ m + 1 − τ m ) ​ J ​ ( K τ m , Θ ⋆ ) \displaystyle=-(\tau_{m+1}-\tau_{m})J(K_{\tau_{m}},\Theta_{\star}) + tr ⁡ ( ( Q + K τ m ⊤ ​ R ​ K τ m ) ​ ∑ t = τ m τ m + 1 − 1 ( x t ​ x t ⊤ − Σ Θ ⋆ K τ m + Σ Θ ⋆ K τ m ) ) \displaystyle\hskip-10.00002pt+\operatorname{\mathrm{tr}}\left((Q+K_{\tau_{m}}^{\top}RK_{\tau_{m}})\sum_{t=\tau_{m}}^{\tau_{m+1}-1}(x_{t}x_{t}^{\top}-\Sigma^{K_{\tau_{m}}}_{\Theta_{\star}}+\Sigma^{K_{\tau_{m}}}_{\Theta_{\star}})\right) ≤ − ( τ m + 1 − τ m ) ​ J ​ ( K τ m , Θ ⋆ ) \displaystyle\leq-(\tau_{m+1}-\tau_{m})J(K_{\tau_{m}},\Theta_{\star}) + ∑ t = τ m τ m + 1 − 1 tr ⁡ ( diag ⁡ ( Q , R ) ​ Σ ~ Θ ⋆ K τ m ) \displaystyle+\sum_{t=\tau_{m}}^{\tau_{m+1}-1}\operatorname{\mathrm{tr}}(\operatorname{diag}(Q,R)\tilde{\Sigma}^{K_{\tau_{m}}}_{\Theta_{\star}}) + ∥ ( Q + K τ m ⊤ R K τ m ) ∥ F ∥ ∑ t = τ m τ m + 1 − 1 ( x t x t ⊤ − Σ ( Θ ⋆ , K τ m ) ∥ F \displaystyle+\left\|(Q+K_{\tau_{m}}^{\top}RK_{\tau_{m}})\right\|_{\mathrm{F}}\left\|\sum_{t=\tau_{m}}^{\tau_{m+1}-1}(x_{t}x_{t}^{\top}-\Sigma(\Theta_{\star},K_{\tau_{m}})\right\|_{\mathrm{F}} ≤ ( ‖ Q ‖ F + ‖ R ‖ F ​ K ¯ 2 ) ​ α ​ ( δ m , τ m + 1 ) ​ τ m + 1 − τ m , \displaystyle\leq(\left\|Q\right\|_{\mathrm{F}}+\left\|R\right\|_{\mathrm{F}}\bar{K}^{2})\alpha(\delta_{m},\tau_{m+1})\sqrt{\tau_{m+1}-\tau_{m}}, where the last inequality is due to the covariance concentration event ℰ m conc \mathcal{E}^{\mathrm{conc}}_{m} . Combined with Corollary VI.1 , this implies that Δ m = 𝒪 ~ ​ ( τ m + 1 − τ m ) \Delta_{m}=\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{\tau_{m+1}-\tau_{m}}) , furthermore, ∑ m = 0 M − 1 τ m + 1 − τ m ≤ M ​ T \sum_{m=0}^{M-1}\sqrt{\tau_{m+1}-\tau_{m}}\leq\sqrt{M}\sqrt{T} . Due to the information gain criterion in line 5 of Algorithm 1 , det ⁡ ( V T ) = det ⁡ ( V τ M ) ≥ 2 M ​ det ⁡ ( V 0 ) = 2 M ​ det ⁡ ( λ ​ I ) \operatorname{\mathrm{det}}(V_{T})=\operatorname{\mathrm{det}}(V_{\tau_{M}})\geq 2^{M}\operatorname{\mathrm{det}}(V_{0})=2^{M}\operatorname{\mathrm{det}}(\lambda I) , therefore ( 2 1 / ( d 𝗑 + d 𝗎 ) ) M ​ λ \displaystyle(2^{1/(d_{\mathsf{x}}+d_{\mathsf{u}})})^{M}\lambda ≤ det ( V T ) 1 / ( d 𝗑 + d 𝗎 ) \displaystyle\leq\operatorname{\mathrm{det}}(V_{T})^{1/(d_{\mathsf{x}}+d_{\mathsf{u}})} ≤ ‖ V T ‖ 2 ≤ ( 1 + K ¯ 2 ) ​ x ¯ T 2 ​ T + λ , \displaystyle\leq\left\|V_{T}\right\|_{2}\leq(1+\bar{K}^{2})\bar{x}_{T}^{2}T+\lambda, resulting in M = 𝒪 ​ ( log ⁡ T ) M=\mathcal{O}(\log T) . Consequently, ℛ T ( 1 ) = 𝒪 ~ ​ ( T ) \mathcal{R}^{(1)}_{T}=\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{T}) . 2. Bounding ℛ T ( 2 ) \mathcal{R}^{(2)}_{T} . Due to Lemma VI.2 , J ​ ( K τ m , Θ ⋆ ) − J ​ ( K τ m , Θ ^ τ m ) \displaystyle J(K_{\tau_{m}},\Theta_{\star})-J(K_{\tau_{m}},\hat{\Theta}_{\tau_{m}}) ≤ tr ⁡ ( 𝒱 τ m ​ Σ ~ Θ ⋆ K τ m ) \displaystyle\leq\operatorname{\mathrm{tr}}(\mathcal{V}_{\tau_{m}}\tilde{\Sigma}^{K_{\tau_{m}}}_{\Theta_{\star}}) ≤ g τ m ​ tr ⁡ ( V τ m − 1 ​ Σ ~ Θ ⋆ K τ m ) . \displaystyle\leq g_{\tau_{m}}\operatorname{\mathrm{tr}}(V_{\tau_{m}}^{-1}\tilde{\Sigma}^{K_{\tau_{m}}}_{\Theta_{\star}}). Applying Lemma VI.6 and Corollary VI.1 yields ℛ T ( 2 ) = 𝒪 ~ ​ ( g T ) = 𝒪 ~ ​ ( T ) \mathcal{R}^{(2)}_{T}=\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(g_{T})=\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{T}) . 3. Bounding ℛ T ( 3 ) \mathcal{R}^{(3)}_{T} . Note that tr ⁡ ( V τ m − 1 ​ Σ ~ Θ ^ τ m K τ m ) = tr ⁡ ( V τ m − 1 ​ ( Σ ~ Θ ^ τ m K τ m − Σ ~ Θ ⋆ K τ m ) ) + tr ⁡ ( V τ m − 1 ​ Σ ~ Θ ⋆ K τ m ) , \displaystyle\operatorname{\mathrm{tr}}(V_{\tau_{m}}^{-1}\tilde{\Sigma}^{K_{\tau_{m}}}_{\hat{\Theta}_{\tau_{m}}})=\operatorname{\mathrm{tr}}(V_{\tau_{m}}^{-1}(\tilde{\Sigma}^{K_{\tau_{m}}}_{\hat{\Theta}_{\tau_{m}}}-\tilde{\Sigma}^{K_{\tau_{m}}}_{\Theta_{\star}}))+\operatorname{\mathrm{tr}}(V_{\tau_{m}}^{-1}\tilde{\Sigma}^{K_{\tau_{m}}}_{\Theta_{\star}}), where tr ⁡ ( V τ m − 1 ​ ( Σ ~ Θ ^ τ m K τ m − Σ ~ Θ ⋆ K τ m ) ) \displaystyle\operatorname{\mathrm{tr}}(V_{\tau_{m}}^{-1}(\tilde{\Sigma}^{K_{\tau_{m}}}_{\hat{\Theta}_{\tau_{m}}}-\tilde{\Sigma}^{K_{\tau_{m}}}_{\Theta_{\star}})) ≤ ‖ Σ Θ ^ τ m K τ m − Σ Θ ⋆ K τ m ‖ F ​ ‖ [ I K τ m ] ⊤ ​ V τ m − 1 ​ [ I K τ m ] ‖ F . \displaystyle\leq\big\|\Sigma^{K_{\tau_{m}}}_{\hat{\Theta}_{\tau_{m}}}-\Sigma^{K_{\tau_{m}}}_{\Theta_{\star}}\big\|_{\mathrm{F}}\left\|\begin{bmatrix}I\\
K_{\tau_{m}}\end{bmatrix}^{\top}V_{\tau_{m}}^{-1}\begin{bmatrix}I\\
K_{\tau_{m}}\end{bmatrix}\right\|_{\mathrm{F}}. Using similar arguments as in the proof of Lemma VI.2 , we get that ‖ Σ Θ ⋆ K − Σ Θ ^ t K ‖ F \displaystyle\big\|\Sigma^{K}_{\Theta_{\star}}-\Sigma^{K}_{\hat{\Theta}_{t}}\big\|_{\mathrm{F}} ≤ C 2 1 − ρ 2 ( 2 ∥ Θ ^ t ∥ F ∥ Σ ~ Θ ⋆ K ( Θ ⋆ − Θ ^ t ) ⊤ ∥ F \displaystyle\leq\frac{C^{2}}{1-\rho^{2}}\Big(2\big\|\hat{\Theta}_{t}\big\|_{\mathrm{F}}\big\|\tilde{\Sigma}^{K}_{\Theta_{\star}}(\Theta^{\star}-\hat{\Theta}_{t})^{\top}\big\|_{\mathrm{F}} + ∥ ( Θ ⋆ − Θ ^ t ) Σ ~ Θ ⋆ K ( Θ ⋆ − Θ ^ t ) ⊤ ∥ F ) \displaystyle+\big\|(\Theta^{\star}-\hat{\Theta}_{t})\tilde{\Sigma}^{K}_{\Theta_{\star}}(\Theta^{\star}-\hat{\Theta}_{t})^{\top}\big\|_{\mathrm{F}}\Big) ≤ 1 λ ​ ( ‖ Θ ^ 0 ‖ F + 2 λ ) ​ 4 ​ C 2 1 − ρ 2 ​ tr ⁡ ( Σ Θ ⋆ K τ m ) \displaystyle\leq\frac{1}{\lambda}\left(\big\|\hat{\Theta}_{0}\big\|_{\mathrm{F}}+\frac{2}{\lambda}\right)\frac{4C^{2}}{1-\rho^{2}}\operatorname{\mathrm{tr}}(\Sigma^{K_{\tau_{m}}}_{\Theta_{\star}}) = c ​ ( λ ) ​ tr ⁡ ( Σ ~ Θ ⋆ K τ m ) / ( ‖ Q ‖ F + K ¯ 2 ​ ‖ R ‖ F ) . \displaystyle=c(\lambda)\operatorname{\mathrm{tr}}(\tilde{\Sigma}^{K_{\tau_{m}}}_{\Theta_{\star}})/(\left\|Q\right\|_{\mathrm{F}}+\bar{K}^{2}\left\|R\right\|_{\mathrm{F}}). Furthermore, ‖ [ I K τ m ] ⊤ ​ V τ m − 1 ​ [ I K τ m ] ‖ F \displaystyle\left\|\begin{bmatrix}I\\
K_{\tau_{m}}\end{bmatrix}^{\top}V_{\tau_{m}}^{-1}\begin{bmatrix}I\\
K_{\tau_{m}}\end{bmatrix}\right\|_{\mathrm{F}} ≤ tr ⁡ ( [ I K τ m ] ⊤ ​ V τ m − 1 ​ [ I K τ m ] ) \displaystyle\leq\operatorname{\mathrm{tr}}\left(\begin{bmatrix}I\\
K_{\tau_{m}}\end{bmatrix}^{\top}V_{\tau_{m}}^{-1}\begin{bmatrix}I\\
K_{\tau_{m}}\end{bmatrix}\right) ≤ 1 σ 𝗐 2 ​ tr ⁡ ( V τ m − 1 ​ Σ ~ Θ ⋆ K τ m ) . \displaystyle\leq\frac{1}{\sigma_{\mathsf{w}}^{2}}\operatorname{\mathrm{tr}}(V_{\tau_{m}}^{-1}\tilde{\Sigma}^{K_{\tau_{m}}}_{\Theta_{\star}}). Thus, tr ⁡ ( 𝒱 τ m ​ Σ ~ Θ ^ τ m K τ m ) ≤ g τ m ​ tr ⁡ ( V τ m − 1 ​ Σ ~ Θ ^ τ m K τ m ) \displaystyle\operatorname{\mathrm{tr}}(\mathcal{V}_{\tau_{m}}\tilde{\Sigma}^{K_{\tau_{m}}}_{\hat{\Theta}_{\tau_{m}}})\leq g_{\tau_{m}}\operatorname{\mathrm{tr}}(V_{\tau_{m}}^{-1}\tilde{\Sigma}^{K_{\tau_{m}}}_{\hat{\Theta}_{\tau_{m}}}) ≤ g τ m ​ ( 1 + c ​ ( λ ) ​ tr ⁡ ( Σ ~ Θ ⋆ K τ m ) σ 𝗐 2 ​ ( ‖ Q ‖ F + K ¯ 2 ​ ‖ R ‖ F ) ) ​ tr ⁡ ( V τ m − 1 ​ Σ ~ Θ ⋆ K τ m ) . \displaystyle\leq g_{\tau_{m}}\left(1+\frac{c(\lambda)\operatorname{\mathrm{tr}}(\tilde{\Sigma}^{K_{\tau_{m}}}_{\Theta_{\star}})}{\sigma_{\mathsf{w}}^{2}(\left\|Q\right\|_{\mathrm{F}}+\bar{K}^{2}\left\|R\right\|_{\mathrm{F}})}\right)\operatorname{\mathrm{tr}}(V_{\tau_{m}}^{-1}\tilde{\Sigma}^{K_{\tau_{m}}}_{\Theta_{\star}}). Lemma VI.6 together with Corollary VI.1 results in ℛ T ( 3 ) = 𝒪 ~ ​ ( g T ) = 𝒪 ~ ​ ( T ) \mathcal{R}^{(3)}_{T}=\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(g_{T})=\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{T}) .

In summary, we have shown that event ℰ \mathcal{E} occurs with probability at least 1 − δ 1-\delta , and the cumulative regret is ℛ T ≤ ℛ T ( 1 ) + ℛ T ( 2 ) + ℛ T ( 3 ) = 𝒪 ~ ​ ( T ) \mathcal{R}_{T}\leq\mathcal{R}^{(1)}_{T}+\mathcal{R}^{(2)}_{T}+\mathcal{R}^{(3)}_{T}=\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{T}) under event ℰ \mathcal{E} . ■ \blacksquare

figures.caption: Figure 1 : Comparison of online LQR methods on the aircraft pitch angle control (top) and unmanned aerial vehicle (bottom) examples, in terms of cumulative regret (left), number of controller updates (middle), and wall-clock time per controller update (right). The median is shown together with bootstrap confidence intervals (60%) across runs. (40 samples, T = 200 T=200 )
